items.q: Were the gospels written down as eyewitness diaries at the time of the events?
items.correct: No - they were composed decades later (Mark is commonly dated around AD 65-75), drawing on oral tradition and earlier sources shaped for particular communities.
items.misconception: The gospels were written word for word as Jesus spoke, like court transcripts.
items.random_wrong: The gospels were written a thousand years after Jesus.
items.why: Understanding the gap between events and writing is the starting point for source criticism and the synoptic problem.

items.q: Do differences between the synoptic gospels mean random copying errors?
items.correct: Not usually - each evangelist selected, arranged and emphasised material for a particular audience and theological purpose, which redaction criticism studies.
items.misconception: Any difference between Matthew, Mark and Luke is just an accidental mistake.
items.random_wrong: The three gospels were written by the same person under different names.
items.why: Treating differences as noise misses the editorial intent that exam questions on themes are built around.

items.q: Are parables simply children's stories with easy morals?
items.correct: No - parables are often subversive, demand a decision about the kingdom, and Mark 4 even presents them as concealing as well as revealing meaning.
items.misconception: Parables are straightforward moral fables that anyone immediately understands.
items.random_wrong: Parables are historical reports of real named individuals.
items.why: Reading parables as bland morality tales strips out the challenge and crisis that synoptic scholarship emphasises.

items.q: Which gospels are known as the synoptic gospels?
items.why: It's Matthew, Mark and Luke. “Matthew, Mark and John” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Mark was written first and used as a source by Matthew and Luke
items.misconception: Mark is the most theologically important gospel
items.random_wrong: Mark was written by Peter himself
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mark was written first and used as a source by Matthew and Luke. “Mark is the most theologically important gospel” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A hypothesised sayings source behind material common to Matthew and Luke but not found in Mark
items.misconception: The oldest complete manuscript of Mark
items.random_wrong: A lost gospel quoted in John
items.why: Correct answer: A hypothesised sayings source behind material common to Matthew and Luke but not found in Mark. “The oldest complete manuscript of Mark” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the parable of the sower about?
items.correct: Different responses to the message of the kingdom
items.misconception: The return of a lost son
items.why: It's Different responses to the message of the kingdom. “The return of a lost son” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
